[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- flower color: pink/magenta
- number of flowers: two
- petal shape: irregular, somewhat elongated, not perfectly symmetrical
- center of flower: visible stamens and pistil, not a distinct yellow disc
- leaves: green, oval, visible around the flowers
</features>
<kb>
- IF [flower color is pink/magenta AND petal shape is irregular AND center of flower shows visible stamens/pistil without a distinct yellow disc] THEN [71]
- IF [flower color is white AND center of flower has a distinct yellow and green disc] THEN [68]
- IF [flower color is pink/magenta AND petal shape is regular and symmetrical AND center of flower has a distinct yellow disc] THEN [33]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 71 fires because the query image shows flowers that are pink/magenta, have irregular petal shapes, and the center of the flower displays visible stamens and pistil without a distinct yellow disc, which matches the features observed in the example for class 71.
</rule_check>
<response>71</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- petal color: white
- number of petals: approximately 5-7 visible per flower
- center color: yellow stamens with a green pistil
- flower arrangement: multiple flowers on a single plant
- leaf shape: deeply lobed or dissected
</features>
<kb>
- IF [petal color = pink AND number of petals = 5-7 AND center color = pink/red] THEN [71]
- IF [petal color = white AND number of petals = 5-7 AND center color = yellow stamens with green pistil] THEN [68]
- IF [petal color = magenta AND number of petals = 8 AND center color = yellow] THEN [33]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 68 fires because the Query Image shows flowers with white petals, approximately 5-7 petals per flower, and a center with yellow stamens and a green pistil.
</rule_check>
<response>68</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- petal color: pink
- petal count: approximately 8
- petal shape: broad, slightly overlapping, with some visible veins
- center color: yellow
- center shape: circular, with small, dense structures
- leaf type: feathery, needle-like, green
- stem: thin, green
- buds: present, small, green, pointed
</features>
<kb>
- IF [petal color = pink AND petal count = approximately 8 AND center color = yellow AND leaf type = feathery, needle-like] THEN [33]
- IF [petal color = pink AND petal count = 5 AND center color = red/pink AND leaf type = broad, dark green] THEN [71]
- IF [petal color = white AND petal count = 6 AND center color = yellow and green AND leaf type = broad, green] THEN [68]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 33 fires because the query image has pink petals, approximately 8 petals, a yellow center, and feathery, needle-like leaves, which matches the conditions for class 33.
</rule_check>
<response>33</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

Valid labels: [71, 68, 33]
Follow the exact XML structure required by the system instructions.
